coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence properties of carbon dots synthesized by different solvents for pH detector
coredata.dc:creator: Huo, Xiaomin
Shen, Honglie
Xu, Yajun
Shao, Jing
Liu, Rui
Zhang, Zhiyong
coredata.dc:description: The carbon dots (CDs) are synthesized by solvothermal method with four kinds of solvents including water, dimethylformamide (DMF), ethanol and acetic acid (AA) reagent. The aqueous solutions of the CDs emit multicolor of blue (470 nm), green (500 nm), yellow (539 nm) and orange (595 nm) with dependence on the excitation wavelength, respectively. The structure, size and chemical composition of the CDs are characterized by TEM, XRD, FTIR and XPS. And the optical properties of these multicolored CDs are analyzed by UV–Vis absorption and PL spectrum. It is found that DMF and AA solvents could improve the generation of graphitic N in carbon cores and increase the COOH functional group during the synthesis of the CDs, which are the key to emit strong red fluorescence. And the COOH groups on the surface of the CDs are beneficial to the application for the determination of pH values in a strong alkaline solution.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots (CDs)
Solvothermal
Functional group
Fluorescence
pH value

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The different pH value solutions for the CDs
Characterizations and optical properties of the multicolored CDs
pH values affected the absorption spectra of the multicolored CDs
The mechanism for solvent effect on synthesis of the CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A schematic illustration of the typical experimental measures for the synthesis of the CDs.
The XPS spectra of the CDs ((a) CDs-B, (b) CDs-G, (c) CDs-Y, (d) CDs-R) synthesized by a solvothermal method in the four solvents.

The PL spectra of the (a) CDs-B, (b) CDs-G, (c) CDs-Y and (d) CDs-R solutions (inner: the photos of the four CDs solutions shone under sunlight (left) and UV light (right)) and the HRTEM images of the (a1) CDs-B, (b1) CDs-G, (c1) CDs-Y and (d1) CDs-R synthesized by solvothermal method in four solvents.
The UV–Vis absorption of the CDs ((a): CDs-B; (b): CDs-G; (c): CDs-Y; (d): CDs-R) synthesized by solvothermal method in four solvents.
The schematic illustration of the fluorescence mechanism within different pH values solutions for the CDs based on the synthesis solvents.

head.ce:title: Fluorescence properties of carbon dots synthesized by different solvents for pH detector

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Technology, Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Materials and Technology for Energy Conversion, Nanjing University of Aeronautics and Astronautics, Nanjing, 210016, PR China

Nanjing University of Aeronautics and Astronautics

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Dalian Product Quality Inspection and Testing Institute Co.

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Dalian Product Quality Inspection and Testing Institute Co., Ltd., Dalian, 116021, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The carbon dots (CDs) are synthesized by solvothermal method with four kinds of solvents including water, dimethylformamide (DMF), ethanol and acetic acid (AA) reagent. The aqueous solutions of the CDs emit multicolor of blue (470 nm), green (500 nm), yellow (539 nm) and orange (595 nm) with dependence on the excitation wavelength, respectively. The structure, size and chemical composition of the CDs are characterized by TEM, XRD, FTIR and XPS. And the optical properties of these multicolored CDs are analyzed by UV–Vis absorption and PL spectrum. It is found that DMF and AA solvents could improve the generation of graphitic N in carbon cores and increase the COOH functional group during the synthesis of the CDs, which are the key to emit strong red fluorescence. And the COOH groups on the surface of the CDs are beneficial to the application for the determination of pH values in a strong alkaline solution.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a new sort of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with particle sizes less than 10 nm [1]. According to the structure, CDs include the carbon nanodots (CNDs) [2,3], graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [4,5] and polymer dots (PDs) [6,7]. CDs are low toxic，environmentally friendly and biocompatible nanomaterials. And they are also used as fluorescent materials due to their excellent optical properties [8,9] such as anti-photobleaching, light stability, no light flicker and wide excitation wavelength range [10]. The fluorescence intensity of CDs is closely related to the environment, and the interaction between CDs and chemical substances can also cause fluorescence quenching or enhancement [11]. They have a wide application for optoelectronic devices [12,13], sensing [14–16], photocatalysis [17], analysis and detection [18,19]. Especially, CDs can be used as a fluorescent probes to detect the pH values [20,21]. Huang et al. used citric acid and basic fuchsin to prepare the CDs by hydrothermal method, which was applied as fluorescent pH sensor [22]. It indicates the successful introduction of C–N/N–H groups to the CDs which have two fluorescence emission peaks, where the wavelengths were 475 nm and 545 nm excited under the UV of 380 nm. The experiment showed that the intensity of the emission peak continuously increased with the pH values from 4.0 to 11.0. The ratio of the emission intensity at 475 nm and 545 nm is in a linear relationship of the pH range from 5.1 to 8.8. Song et al. proposed a dual functional probe with the CDs [23]. It is ascribed that PO and P–C/P–N groups could control the fluorescent properties of CDs. The CDs had two emission peaks at 400 nm with a blue light and 624 nm with a red light. The blue light emission peak of the CDs was dependent of arginine and the intensity was stable when the pH value was in the range between 1.5 and 5.0. While the red-light emission peak was independent on arginine, and the intensity decreased with the pH values from 1.5 to 5.0.

The pH sensors based on CDs are currently used in acid or neutral solutions [24,25]. It is very difficult to make use of fluorescence features to improve the sensing performance of CDs in strong alkali solutions. At present, there are three kinds of fluorescence mechanisms such as quantum effect, surface state and compound radiation of electron-hole [26]. The characteristics of photoluminescence (PL) can reflect the quantum confinement effect and surface trap state of CDs [27]. Therefore, the size, the content of graphite nitrogen and the functional groups on the surface of CDs can affect the fluorescence spectrum [28,29]. They could play an important role in the performance of the pH sensor. The hydrothermal method is simple, low cost and environment friendly to prepare CDs [30,31]. And it is found that nitrogen doping could improve the fluorescence yield of CDs [32–34]. In this paper, the CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method with citric acid as the carbon source and urea as the nitrogen source in four solvents that have different characteristic functional groups. The four solvents are water, dimethylformamide (DMF), ethanol and acetic acid (AA). The size of CDs can be adjusted by dialysis centrifugal treatment. The structure, size and chemical composition of the as-prepared CDs were characterized by TEM, XRD, FTIR and XPS. The UV absorption and fluorescence emission of them were studied systematically. The CDs were used as pH fluorescence sensors in acid and alkaline solutions. Our aim is to research that the fluorescence mechanism of corresponding functional groups’ effects on the determination of the pH values.

The citric acid (CA), urea, dimethylformamide (DMF), acetic acid (AA), ethanol, sodium borohydride (NaBH4), sodium hydroxide and hydrochloric acid were purchased from Nanjing Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Dialysis bag (MD44-1000D) and microporous membrane (0.22 μm) were obtained from Beijing Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd. (China). They didn't need purification. The ultra-pure water was prepared by a laboratory pure water machine (Unique-R20, RSJ Technology Co., Ltd.).

The carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized by hydrothermal method using citric acid as the carbon source and urea as the nitrogen source in different solvents. 2.882 g citric acid and 1.201 g urea were dissolved in 20.0 mL ultra-pure water (for CDs-B), 20.0 mL DMF (for CDs-G), 10.0 mL DMF and 10.0 mL ethanol (for CDs-Y), 10.0 mL DMF and 10.0 mL AA (for CDs-R), respectively. The mixed solutions were stirred evenly after the ultrasound and transferred into 50 mL Teflon lined autoclaves and were kept at 200 °C for 6 h. After the reaction, the mixtures were cooled at room temperature to obtain carbon dots solutions. They were dialyzed against ultra-pure water with a dialysis bag (1000D) by ultrasound for 72 h. Then, the upper liquid was collected after the high-speed centrifuge at 12,000 rpm for 30 min and filtered with a microporous membrane (0.22 μm). The powders of CDs were obtained by the solutions dried in a vacuum at 40 °C for overnight, which were named CDs-B, CDs-G, CDs-Y and CDs-R, respectively. It should be noted that CDs need to be stored in a vacuum to prevent from sunlight and oxidation. A schematic illustration of the typical experimental measures for the synthesis of the CDs is shown in Fig. 1 .

Transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEM 2100F, JEOL) was used to analyze the morphology of CDs. X-ray diffraction (XRD, D8 Advance, Bruker), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Escalab 250Xi, Thermo Fisher) and Fourier Transform Infra-Red (FTIR, Nicolet iS 10, Thermo Fisher) analyzed the structure and the functional groups of CDs.

The optical properties of CDs were determined by UV–Vis spectrum (D7, Runqee Instrument Technology Co., Ltd.) and photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL, QM40-NIR, PTI). All tests were carried out at room temperature. The quantum yeild (QY) of the CDs were determined according to quinine sulfate (QY
R = 54%). The QY of CDs can be calculated from equation below:
(1)
QY =
QY R
×
m
m R
×
n 2
n R 2
× 100
where QY is quantum yield of the CDs in %, QY
R is quantum yield of quinine sulfate in 54%, m is the slope of the plot of integrated emitting fluorescence intensity vs. Absorbance for the CDs, m
R is the slope of the plot of integrated emitting fluorescence intensity vs. Absorbance for quinine sulfate, n is the refractive index of the CDs in 1.33, n
R is the refractive index of quinine sulfate in 1.60.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The different pH value solutions for the CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The solution with a pH value of 2 was prepared by 50 μL of 38% hydrochloric acid diluting into 60.0 mL with ultrapure water. The pH values of 3, 4, 5 and 6 were used by 1.0 mL of the solution with a pH value of 2 diluting into 10, 100, 1000 and 10,000 mL, respectively. The solution with a pH value of 12 was that 20 mg of sodium hydroxide were dissolved in 50.0 mL ultrapure water. The pH values of 11, 10, 9 and 8 were also prepared by 1.0 mL of the solution with a pH value of 12 diluting into 10, 100, 1000 and 10,000 mL, respectively. The 1.0 mg of four CDs were dispersed into 100.0 mL the solutions with different pH values, respectively. They were named CDs-pH (such as CDs-B-pH2, CDs-B-pH3, CDs-B-pH4 et al.). The real data of pH value for these solutions were tested by acidometer (Shanghai Rex Instrument Factory, PHS-3C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations and optical properties of the multicolored CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The XPS spectra were used to characterize the surface chemical composition and content analysis of the CDs as shown in Fig. 2 . The four CDs mainly contain the C, N and O elements. The C1s spectrum consists of three peaks at 284.8, 286.8 and 288.6 eV pertaining to the C–C/CC, C–N/C–O and CO bonds in the four CDs. While, the peaks corresponding to the COOH component in CDs-G, CDs-Y and CDs-R exist at 290.2 eV in the spectra of Fig. 2(b), (c) and (d). The content of the COOH group is the highest in CDs-R. The FTIR spectrum of CDs-R has contained the absorption peaks of the CO and C–O functional groups yet, which are also observed in CDs-G and -Y as shown in Fig. S1(b). In Fig. 2, the N1s spectrum had a binding energy value of 399.5 and 401.1 eV assigned to pyrrole-like N and graphitic N [35,36]. The percent of the N element increases from 8.4 to 14.6% attribute to DMF reagent instead of water. The percent of the N element for CDs-Y is 16.3% synthesized by the reagents of DMF and ethanol, which is higher than those for CDs-G and CDs-R. It could be contributed to the solvent effect of ethanol to improve the nitrogen functional groups. While, the contents of graphitic N in the N1s spectra of CDs-G, CDs-Y and CDs-R are 6.6, 9.7 and 16.6%, respectively, which are calculated by the peak intensity of the pyrrole-like N and graphitic N. It is also observed that the intensity of CN (1625 cm−1) and C–N (1489 cm−1) absorption peaks increased slightly as shown in The FTIR spectrum (Fig. S1(b)). Fig. S1(a) shows the XRD patterns of the four CDs. It is exhibited that the four CDs have a wide diffraction peak near 27.0° corresponding to the (002) crystal plane characteristic diffraction peak of graphite. It indicates that the degree of graphitization increases. The O1s spectrum consists of two components such as the CO and C–O groups located at 531.0 and 532.3 eV in the XPS spectra (Fig. 2) [37,38]. The percent of the O element decreases from 28.8 to 20.9% in turn. It is observed that the CO functional groups dominate the surface of CDs-B. But the C–O–C and COOH groups instead of the CO groups increase on the surface on the CDs-G, -Y and –R. It is inferred that the functional groups of the CDs such as the CO, C–O–C, COOH and graphitic N are affected by DMF solvent mixing ethanol or AA solvent according to the FTIR and XPS spectra.

The PL spectra are determined as shown in Fig. 3 (a), (b), (c) and (d), which research the optical properties of the four CDs solutions. The size and lattice of the four CDs had been determined by HRTEM as shown in Fig. 3(a1), (b1), (c1) and (d1). The average sizes of CDs-B, CDs-G, CDs-Y and –R are about 3.18 nm, 3.25 nm, 3.47 nm and 3.68 nm, respectively. From the HRTEM images, it is clearly shown that the lattice structures of these CDs have lattice stripes of graphite carbon. the crystal spacing of CDs-B synthesized by water is about 0.22 nm, but the crystal spacing of the other three CDs prepared by DMF solvent is 0.21 nm smaller than CDs-B. In addition, the quantum yield (QY) of the four CDs are calculated to be approximately 20.1, 22.1, 21.9 and 24.2%, respectively. The DMF solvent also improves the QY of CDs-G, CDs-Y and CDs-R.

In Fig. 3(a), (b), (c) and (d), a sharp contrast is observed in the inner photos that the four CDs solutions are shone under sunlight (left) and 355 nm laser radiates (right). The solutions of CDs-B, CDs-G and CDs-Y emit bright blue, cyan and light cyan nearly white fluorescence, respectively. While the solution of CDs-R shows the pretty hot-pink. It is also observed that the emission wavelength of the four CDs is dependent on the excitation wavelength. The emission peak of the CDs-B solution is located at near 450 nm under excitation wavelength from 280 to 340 nm which is consistent with the results reported in the literature [39]. It gradually shifts to a longer wavelength along with the excitation wavelength as presented in Fig. 3(a). The maximum emission peak is located at the blue light region of 470 nm with its corresponding excitation wavelength of 400 nm. However, the emission wavelength is only red-shifted to 535 nm with an even longer excitation wavelength. It indicates that the CDs-B solution can't emit red fluorescence.

The emission peak of CDs-G solution shifts red along with the excitation wavelength increasing from 360 to 500 nm as shown in Fig. 3(b). The maximum emission peak is located at the green light region of 500 nm under the excitation wavelength of 420 nm. As the excitation wavelength is between 520 and 560 nm, the emission wavelength exceeds 600 nm. It indicates the fact that CDs-G solution could emit red fluorescence under long wavelength excitation, but the intensity is low. Hence, this variation in the optical properties of the CDs is as certain attributed to the effect of DMF solvent comparing with water. As shown in Fig. 3(c), the PL spectra of the CDs-Y solution shifted red with the excitation wavelength ranging from 360 to 500 nm. The maximum emission peak is located at the yellow light region of 539 nm corresponding to the excitation wavelength of 443 nm. Thus, it is found that DMF solvent added ethanol reagent takes little effect on the maximum emission peak and excitation peak position. It shows that the maximum emission peak of the CDs-R solution is at 595 nm under the excitation wavelength of 560 nm in Fig. 3(d). With the increase of excitation wavelength from 380 to 460 nm, the weak emission peak of CDs-R solution also shifted red. But the intensities of emission peaks rise intensively along with the excitation wavelength between 480 and 560 nm at the similar position of 595 nm. The intensity of red-light emission for CDs-R can be greatly enhanced due to synthesis solvent adding AA reagent. It is an immediate result that the CDs synthesized by DMF solvent or mixed by ethanol and AA reagents can emit red fluorescence. The ethanol reagent weakly affects to emits red light. However, the AA reagent can enhance the intensity of red fluorescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH values affected the absorption spectra of the multicolored CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of pH value in solutions on the functional groups of the CDs is studied. Fig. 4 represents that the UV–Vis absorption spectra of CDs-B, CDs-G, CDs-Y and CDs-R all shift with pH value. The absorption intensity raises along with the pH value randomly, but the absorption wavelength depends on the functional groups of the CDs which are influenced by pH values. The absorption wavelength of CDs-B gradually shifts from 325 to 335 nm with pH values ranging from 2 to 10 shown in Fig. 4(a). It reflects that the electrons from the functional groups of CDs-B can be excited to a high energy level by lower energy. When the pH values are above 10, the absorption wavelength slightly changes towards blue light to 331 nm, revealing that the electrons were bound by OH− in solution need higher energy to transfer. Meanwhile, the absorption wavelength of CDs-G at 556 nm does not change with pH values as presented in Fig. 4(b). The electrons from the functional groups of CDs-G can be excited by lower energy than CDs-B. As shown in Fig. 4(c), the absorption peak of CDs-Y has the same trend as CDs-G except that the wavelength is at 568 nm. But the absorption peak width of CDs-G and -Y narrowed in the visible light region of 600–650 nm can attribute to the transition electrons bound by OH− when the pH values are 10, 11 and 12. It indicates that the energy of visible light above 600 nm affects the transition energy of the electrons in a strong alkaline solution.

As represented in Fig. 4(d), the intensity of CDs-R absorption peak reaches the maximum at pH value of 11. The maximum absorption wavelength keeps at 539 nm below the pH values of 7, and shifts to 527 nm with the pH values ranging from 7 to 12 due to too many COOH groups on the surface of CDs-R. The electrons from the functional groups of CDs-B also need higher energy to excite than the other three CDs. The pH values have effects on the process of protonation-deprotonation and the surface electricity [40]. However, the functional groups of CDs-G, CDs-Y and CDs-R could avoid the interference of H+ below the pH value of 7 which are mainly governed by the COOH groups. It is obvious that the functional groups of the four CDs are bound by OH− in a strong alkaline solution when the pH value is above 10.

The effect of the functional groups on the fluorescence performances of the CDs is measured by PL spectra, which could be applied for the determination of pH values. However, the fluorescence properties are affected by many factors which can be indirectly used as the sensor of pH value. In this work, it uses the ratio between the emission intensity of two wavelengths as the signal to determine the different pH values [22]. Figs. S2 and S3, S4and S5 show the PL spectra of the four CDs in solutions with different pH values and the calibration curves of the ratios versus pH values. It is concluded that the sensitivity of CDs to the pH value in strong alkaline solution could be improved by the content of COOH groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism for solvent effect on synthesis of the CDs
ce:section.ce:para: It is observed that the CO functional groups are dominated on the surface of CDs-B. But the C–O–C and COOH groups increase from CDs-G, CDs-Y to CDs-R instead of the CO groups. It is inferred that the functional groups on the CDs such as the CO, C–O–C, COOH and graphitic N are affected by DMF solvent mixed with ethanol or AA reagent. The DMF solvent added AA reagent accelerates the cleavage of the N–H bond and the formation of the C–N–C bond in carbon cores. Therefore, the contents of graphitic N in carbon cores and COOH functional groups on the surface of the CDs-R both increase significantly, which is the key to adjust emission peaks to red. For example, the functional groups including CO, –OH and –NH2 would generate the force with H+ in an acid solution. In a strong alkaline solution, the COOH groups on the surface of CDs-R can combine with OH−, but the effects of other groups are weakened. The schematic illustration of the fluorescence mechanism for the CDs obtained in different mediums is shown in Fig. 5 .

The four sorts of CDs are synthesized by a solvothermal method in four solvents including water, dimethylformamide (DMF), ethanol and acetic acid (AA). They are CDs-B (blue, 470 nm), CDs-G (green, 500 nm), CDs-Y (yellow, 539 nm) and CDs-R (orange, 595 nm) with different colors and fluorescence emission wavelengths applied for the determination of pH values. It is observed that the CO functional groups are dominated on the surface of CDs-B which can not emit red fluorescence. The C–O–C and COOH groups increasing on the surface of CDs-G, CDs-Y and CDs-R substitute the CO groups, which could benefit the CDs to emit red fluorescence. It is inferred that the contents of graphitic N in carbon cores and COOH functional groups on the surface of the CDs are the key to emit red light. And the functional groups of the CDs such as the CO, C–O–C, COOH and graphitic N are affected by DMF solvent. So DMF solvent is the key solvent for emitting red fluorescence. Once DMF solvent is mixed with acetic acid (AA) reagent, the intensity of red fluorescence emitted by the CDs solution could be enhanced due to the content of COOH groups on the surface of it. It is concluded that the sensitivity of the CDs to the pH value in a strong alkaline solution could be improved by the content of COOH groups. Therefore, CDs-R has a great potential to fabricate a pH sensor for a strong alkaline solution in further research.

Xiaomin Huo: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing. Honglie Shen: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Yajun Xu: Conceptualization, Resources. Jing Shao: Software, Formal analysis. Rui Liu: Validation, Resources. Zhiyong Zhang: Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the National Natural Science Foundation of China, China (61774084), the special fund of Jiangsu province for the transformation of scientific and technological achievements (BA2019047), the open project of Key Laboratory of Materials Preparation and Protection for Harsh Environment, Ministry of Industry and Information Technology, China (XCA20013-3), The Special Scientific Innovation Found of Sihong County (H201901) and the university natural science research project Found of Anhui (KJ2019A0849).
